en.extract:
The Australian ringneck (Barnardius zonarius) is a parrot native to Australia. Except for extreme tropical and highland areas, the species has adapted to all conditions. Treatments of genus Barnardius have previously recognised two species, the Port Lincoln parrot (Barnardius zonarius) and the mallee ringneck (Barnardius barnardi), but due to these readily interbreeding at the contact zone they are usually regarded as a single species B. zonarius with subspecific descriptions. Currently, four subspecies are recognised, each with a distinct range.
In Western Australia, the ringneck competes for nesting space with the rainbow lorikeet, an introduced species.  To protect the ringneck, culls of the lorikeet are sanctioned by authorities in this region. Overall, though, the ringneck is not a threatened species.


Description
The subspecies of the Australian ringneck differ considerably in colouration. It is a medium size species around 33 cm (13 in) long. The basic colour is green, and all four subspecies have the characteristic yellow ring around the hindneck; wings and tail are a mixture of green and blue.

The B. z. zonarius and B. z. semitorquatus subspecies have a dull black head; back, rump and wings are brilliant green; throat and breast bluish-green. The difference between these two subspecies is that B. z. zonarius has a yellow abdomen while B. z. semitorquatus has a green abdomen; the latter has also a prominent crimson frontal band that the former lacks (the intermediate shown in the box has characteristics of both subspecies). The two other subspecies differ from these subspecies by the bright green crown and nape and blush cheek-patches. The underparts of B. z. barnardi are turquoise-green with an irregular orange-yellow band across the abdomen; the back and mantle are deep blackish-blue and this subspecies has a prominent red frontal band. The B. z. macgillivrayi is generally pale green, with no red frontal band, and a wide uniform pale yellow band across the abdomen.
The calls of the Mallee ringneck and Cloncurry parrot have been described as "ringing", and the calls of the Port Lincoln ringneck and Twenty-eight parrot have been described as "strident". The name of the Twenty-eight  is an onomatopoeic derived from its distinctive call, which sounds like "twenty-eight"  (or the French equivalent, vingt-huit, according to one early description).


Taxonomy and naming
The Australian ringneck was first described by English naturalist George Shaw and drawn by Frederick Polydore Nodder in 1805 in their work The Naturalist's Miscellany: Or, Coloured Figures of Natural Objects; Drawn and Described Immediately From Nature. He called it Psittacus zonarius "zoned parrot". A broad-tailed parrot, it is most closely related to the rosellas of the genus Platycercus, and has been placed in that genus by some authorities, including Ferdinand Bauer.
Pre-existing names for the species, derived from the Nyungar language of Southwest Australia, are dowarn [pronounced dow'awn] and doomolok [dorm'awe'lawk]; these were identified from over one hundred records of regional and orthographic variants to supplement the names already suggested by John Gilbert, Dominic Serventy and others.
Currently, four subspecies of ringneck are recognised, all of which have been described as distinct species in the past: (As of 1993, the Twenty-eight and Cloncurry parrot were treated as subspecies of the Port Lincoln parrot and the mallee ringneck, respectively.)
Several other subspecies have been described, but are considered synonyms with one of the above subspecies. B. z. occidentalis has been synonymised with B. z. zonarius. Intermediates exist between all subspecies except for between B. z. zonarius and B. z. macgillivrayi. Intermediates have been associated with land clearing for agriculture in southern Western Australia.
The classification of this species is still debated, and molecular research by Joseph and Wilke in 2006 found that the complex split genetically into two clades—one roughly correlating with B. z. barnardi and the other with the other three forms; B. z. macgillivrayi was more closely related to B. z. zonarius than to the neighbouring B. z. barnardi. The researchers felt it was premature to reorganise the classification of the complex until more study was undertaken.


Subspecies


Behaviour
The Australian ringneck is active during the day and can be found in eucalypt woodlands and eucalypt-lined watercourses. The species is gregarious and depending on the conditions can be resident or nomadic. In trials of growing hybrid eucalypt trees in dry environments parrots, especially the Port Lincoln parrot, caused severe damage to the crowns of the younger trees during the research period between 2000–2003.


Feeding
This species eats a wide range of foods that include nectar, insects, seeds, fruit, and native and introduced bulbs. It will eat orchard-grown fruit and is sometimes seen as a pest by farmers.


Breeding
Breeding season for the northern populations starts in June or July, while the central and southern populations breed from August to December, but this can be delayed when climatic conditions are unfavourable. The nesting site is a hollow in a tree trunk. Generally four or five white oval eggs are laid measuring 29 mm x 23 mm, although a clutch may be as few as three and as many as six. Fledgling survival rates have been measured at 75%.


Conservation
Although the species is endemic, the species is considered not threatened, but in Western Australia, the Twenty-eight subspecies (B. z. semitorquatus) gets locally displaced by the introduced rainbow lorikeets that aggressively compete for nesting places. The rainbow lorikeet is considered a pest species in Western Australia and is subject to eradication in the wild.
In Western Australia, a licence is required to keep or dispose of more than four Port Lincoln ringnecks. All four subspecies are sold in the Canary Islands and in Australia, and they are traded via the CITES convention. The sale of the Cloncurry parrot is restricted in Queensland. The Australian ringneck can suffer from psittacine beak and feather disease, which causes a high nestling mortality rate in captivity.


References


Cited text
Lendon, Alan H. (1973). Australian Parrots in Field and Aviary (2nd ed.). Sydney: Angus and Robertson. ISBN 0-207-12424-8.


Further reading

The Atlas of Australian Birds, Blakers, Davies & Reilly, (1984) ISBN 0-522-84285-2
Photographic Field Guide to Birds of Australia, Jim Flegg, (2002) ISBN 1-876334-78-9
John Gould's The Birds of Australia :
Full text available from National Library of Australia in an electronic format at http://nla.gov.au/nla.aus-f4773
B. z. semitorquatus plate

''B. z. semitorquatus''
''B. z. barnardi''
''B. z. macgillivrayi'' }} The '''Australian ringneck''' ('''''Barnardius zonarius''''') is a [[parrot]] native to [[Australia]]. Except for extreme [[tropical]] and highland areas, the species has adapted to all conditions. Treatments of genus '''''Barnardius''''' have previously recognised two species, the Port Lincoln parrot (''Barnardius zonarius'') and the mallee ringneck (''Barnardius barnardi''),{{cite book | first= Joseph M.| last= Forshaw| author2= Cooper, William T.|year= 1981|orig-date=1973, 1978|edition=corrected second| title= Parrots of the World|publisher=David & Charles, Newton Abbot, London|isbn=0-7153-7698-5}} but due to these readily interbreeding at the contact zone they are usually regarded as a single species ''B. zonarius'' with subspecific descriptions.Christidis, L. & Boles, W.E. (1994). The Taxonomy and Species of Birds of Australia and its Territories. Hawthorn East, Victoria : Royal Ornithologists Union Monograph Vol. 2 112 pp.{{cite journal | last1 = Joseph | first1 = L. | last2 = Wilke | first2 = T. | year = 2006 | title = Molecular resolution of population history, systematics and historical biogeography of the Australian ringneck parrots ''Barnardius'': are we there yet? | url = http://www.publish.csiro.au/nid/96/paper/MU05035.htm| journal = Emu | volume = 106 | issue = 1 | pages = 49–62 | doi = 10.1071/mu05035 | bibcode = 2006EmuAO.106...49J | s2cid = 84278709 | url-access = subscription }} Currently, four [[subspecies]] are recognised, each with a distinct [[range (biology)|range]]. In [[Western Australia]], the ringneck competes for nesting space with the [[rainbow lorikeet]], an [[introduced species]]. To protect the ringneck, culls of the lorikeet are sanctioned by authorities in this region. Overall, though, the ringneck is not a threatened species. ==Description== The subspecies of the Australian ringneck differ considerably in colouration. It is a medium size species around {{cvt|33|cm|in|0}} long. The basic colour is green, and all four subspecies have the characteristic yellow ring around the hindneck; wings and tail are a mixture of green and blue. [[File:Australian Ringneck, Perth.jpg|thumb|left|''B. z. semitorquatus'', [[Perth, Western Australia|Perth]], [[Western Australia]]]] The ''B. z. zonarius'' and ''B. z. semitorquatus'' subspecies have a dull black head; back, rump and wings are brilliant green; throat and breast bluish-green. The difference between these two subspecies is that ''B. z. zonarius'' has a yellow abdomen while ''B. z. semitorquatus'' has a green abdomen; the latter has also a prominent crimson frontal band that the former lacks (the intermediate shown in the box has characteristics of both subspecies). The two other subspecies differ from these subspecies by the bright green crown and nape and blush cheek-patches. The underparts of ''B. z. barnardi'' are turquoise-green with an irregular orange-yellow band across the abdomen; the back and mantle are deep blackish-blue and this subspecies has a prominent red frontal band. The ''B. z. macgillivrayi'' is generally pale green, with no red frontal band, and a wide uniform pale yellow band across the abdomen.{{cite book|title=Field Guide to the Birds of Australia - A book of identification|last1=Simpson|author-link1=Ken Simpson|name-list-style=and|last2=Day|year=1993|page=144|isbn=0-670-90670-0}} The calls of the Mallee ringneck and Cloncurry parrot have been described as "ringing", and the calls of the Port Lincoln ringneck and Twenty-eight parrot have been described as "strident". The name of the Twenty-eight is an [[Onomatopoeia|onomatopoeic]] derived from its distinctive call, which sounds like "twenty-eight" (or the French equivalent, {{Lang|fr|vingt-huit}}, according to one early description).{{cite web |url=http://www.dpi.nsw.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0010/193744/managing_bird_damage-4.pdf |title=Managing bird damage to fruit and other horticultural crops. Part B: Fact sheets for growers|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160616230000/http://www.dpi.nsw.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0010/193744/managing_bird_damage-4.pdf |archive-date=16 June 2016 |publisher= NSW Department of Primary Industries|access-date= 6 August 2013}} ==Taxonomy and naming== The Australian ringneck was first described by English naturalist [[George Shaw (biologist)|George Shaw]] and drawn by [[Frederick Polydore Nodder]] in 1805 in their work ''The Naturalist's Miscellany: Or, Coloured Figures of Natural Objects; Drawn and Described Immediately From Nature''. He called it ''Psittacus zonarius'' "zoned parrot".{{cite book | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/40521247 | volume=16 | pages= pl. 637 | title=The naturalist's miscellany, or Coloured figures of natural objects | author=Shaw, George | publisher=Nodder & Co. | location=London, United Kingdom | year=1805 }} A [[broad-tailed parrot]], it is most closely related to the rosellas of the genus ''[[Platycercus]]'',{{cite journal | last1=Joseph| first1= Leo|first2= Alicia |last2=Toon| first3= Erin E.|last3= Schirtzinger | first4= Timothy F. |last4=Wright| title=Molecular systematics of two enigmatic genera ''Psittacella'' and ''Pezoporus'' illuminate the ecological radiation of Australo-Papuan parrots (Aves: Psittaciformes) | journal= Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution |volume=59 | issue= 3 | year=2011|pages= 675–84 | doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2011.03.017 | pmid= 21453777| bibcode= 2011MolPE..59..675J}} and has been placed in that genus by some authorities, including [[Ferdinand Bauer]].{{cite web|url=http://www.nla.gov.au/pict/list/bauer.html|title=Bauer, Ferdinand, 1760-1826 - natural history drawings|publisher=[[National Library of Australia]] }} Pre-existing names for the species, derived from the [[Nyungar language]] of [[Southwest Australia (ecoregion)|Southwest Australia]], are ''dowarn'' [pronounced dow'awn] and ''doomolok'' [dorm'awe'lawk]; these were identified from over one hundred records of regional and orthographic variants to supplement the names already suggested by [[John Gilbert (naturalist)|John Gilbert]], [[Dominic Serventy]] and others.{{cite journal|author=Abbott, Ian | title=Aboriginal names of bird species in south-west Western Australia, with suggestions for their adoption into common usage |year= 2009 |journal= Conservation Science Western Australia Journal |volume= 7 |issue=2 |pages=254–55 |url=http://www.dpaw.wa.gov.au/images/documents/about/science/cswa/articles/14.pdf}} Currently, four subspecies of ringneck are recognised, all of which have been described as distinct species in the past:{{cite web|url=https://biodiversity.org.au/afd/taxa/Barnardius_zonarius/complete |title=Barnardius zonarius (Shaw, 1805) |access-date=29 October 2016 |work=Australian Biological Resources Study }} (As of 1993, the Twenty-eight and Cloncurry parrot were treated as subspecies of the Port Lincoln parrot and the mallee ringneck, respectively.) Several other subspecies have been described, but are considered synonyms with one of the above subspecies. ''B. z. occidentalis'' has been synonymised with ''B. z. zonarius''.[[Richard Schodde|Schodde, R.]] & [[Ian J. Mason|Mason, I.J.]] (1997) [[Aves]] ([[Columbidae]] to [[Coraciidae]]). In, Houston, W.W.K. & Wells, A. (eds) Zoological Catalogue of Australia. Melbourne: CSIRO Publishing, Australia Vol. 37.2 xiii 440 pp. Intermediates exist between all subspecies except for between ''B. z. zonarius'' and ''B. z. macgillivrayi''.{{cite journal|doi=10.1071/MU9870158|last=Ford|first= J.|year=1987|title=Hybrid zones in Australian birds|journal=Emu|volume= 87|pages=158–178|url=http://www.publish.csiro.au/nid/96/paper/MU9870158.htm|issue=3|bibcode=1987EmuAO..87..158F |url-access=subscription}} Intermediates have been associated with land clearing for agriculture in southern Western Australia. The classification of this species is still debated, and molecular research by Joseph and Wilke in 2006 found that the complex split genetically into two clades—one roughly correlating with ''B. z. barnardi'' and the other with the other three forms; ''B. z. macgillivrayi'' was more closely related to ''B. z. zonarius'' than to the neighbouring ''B. z. barnardi''. The researchers felt it was premature to reorganise the classification of the complex until more study was undertaken. ===Subspecies=== {| width = 98% class="wikitable" ! align="center" style="background-color: #D3D3A4" colspan ="4" |'''Subspecies''' |- ! width = "20%" |Common and binomial names ! Image ! align="center"|Description ! width = "28%" |Range |- |Twenty-eight parrot |rowspan="2"|{{center|[[File:Barnardius zonarius semitorquatus -2c.jpg|500x180px]]}} |rowspan="2"|Identification: The red band and green belly distinguishes it from the Port Lincoln parrot. |rowspan="2"|Found in the south western forests of coastal and subcoastal Western Australia.Lendon, p. 166 |- | ''B. z. semitorquatus''
([[Jean René Constant Quoy|Quoy]] & [[Joseph Paul Gaimard|Gaimard]], 1830) |- |Port Lincoln parrot or
Port Lincoln ringneck |rowspan="2"|{{center|[[File:Barnardius zonarius (Wroclaw zoo)-1.JPG|500x180px]]}} |rowspan="2"| |rowspan="2"|Common from [[Port Lincoln, South Australia|Port Lincoln]] in the south east to [[Alice Springs]] in the north east, and from the Karri and Tingle forests of South Western Australia up to the [[Pilbara]] district.Lendon, p. 161 |- | ''B. z. zonarius''
([[George Shaw (biologist)|Shaw]], 1805)
 |- |Cloncurry parrot |rowspan="2"|{{center|[[File:Barnardius zonarius macgillivrayi - Buffalo Zoo.jpg|500x180px]]}} |rowspan="2"|Identification: The yellow belly, lighter green colour and lack of red band distinguishes it from the mallee ringneck. |rowspan="2"|Found from the [[Lake Eyre]] basin in the [[Northern Territory]] to the [[Gulf Country]] of northwestern Queensland, from [[Burketown, Queensland|Burketown]] south to [[Boulia, Queensland|Boulia]], with [[Kynuna]] and [[Camooweal, Queensland|Camooweel]] as eastern and western limits respectively.Lendon, p. 157 |- |''B. z. macgillivrayi''
([[Alfred John North|North]], 1900) |- |Mallee ringneck |rowspan="2"|{{center|[[File:Mallee ringneck - Patchewollock Conservation Reserve.jpg|150px]]}} |rowspan="2"| |rowspan="2"|Inhabits central and western [[New South Wales]] west of [[Dubbo]], the southwestern corner [[Queensland]] west of [[St George, Queensland|St George]], eastern [[South Australia]] and northwestern [[Victoria (Australia)|Victoria]].Lendon, pp. 152–52 |- |''B. z. barnardi''
([[Nicholas Aylward Vigors|Vigors]] & [[Thomas Horsfield|Horsfield]], 1827) |} ==Behaviour== The Australian ringneck is active during the day and can be found in [[Eucalyptus|eucalypt]] woodlands and eucalypt-lined watercourses. The species is gregarious and depending on the conditions can be resident or nomadic. In trials of growing hybrid eucalypt trees in dry environments parrots, especially the Port Lincoln parrot, caused severe damage to the crowns of the younger trees during the research period between 2000–2003.{{cite web|url=http://www.rirdc.gov.au/reports/AFT/04-021sum.html|year=2004|last=Barbour|first=E.L.|title=Eucalypt hybrids in south-west Western Australia|publisher=[[RIRDC]], Australian government|access-date=2008-08-02 |archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20071005132142/http://www.rirdc.gov.au/reports/AFT/04-021sum.html |archive-date = 5 October 2007}} ===Feeding=== This species eats a wide range of foods that include nectar, insects, seeds, fruit, and native and introduced bulbs. It will eat orchard-grown fruit and is sometimes seen as a pest by farmers.{{cite web|url=http://www.agric.wa.gov.au/content/LWE/VEGT/TREES/TREENOTE26.htm|publisher=Department of Agriculture and Food, Western Australia|title=Parrot damage in agroforestry in the greater than 450 mm rainfall zone of Western Australia|access-date=2007-11-07|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071123160945/http://www.agric.wa.gov.au/content/LWE/VEGT/TREES/TREENOTE26.htm|archive-date=23 November 2007}} ===Breeding=== Breeding season for the northern populations starts in June or July, while the central and southern populations breed from August to December, but this can be delayed when climatic conditions are unfavourable. The nesting site is a hollow in a tree trunk.{{cite web|url=http://www.birdsinbackyards.net/finder/display.cfm?id=362 |title=Australian Ringneck |publisher=[[Australian Museum]] |work=birdsinbackyards.net |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080430081837/http://www.birdsinbackyards.net/finder/display.cfm?id=362 |archive-date=30 April 2008}} Generally four or five white oval eggs are laid measuring 29 mm x 23 mm, although a clutch may be as few as three and as many as six.{{cite book | last = Beruldsen | first = G | title = Australian Birds: Their Nests and Eggs | publisher = self | year = 2003 | location = Kenmore Hills, Qld| isbn = 0-646-42798-9 | page = 247}} Fledgling survival rates have been measured at 75%.{{cite web|url=http://www.agric.wa.gov.au/content/pw/vp/bird/22_australian_ringneck.pdf|title=Australian ringneck|publisher=Department of Agriculture and Food, Western Australia|work=Fauna Note No. 22|access-date=2008-08-02|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080901134258/http://www.agric.wa.gov.au/content/pw/vp/bird/22_australian_ringneck.pdf|archive-date=1 September 2008}} ==Conservation== Although the species is endemic,{{cite journal|last=Martin|first=Stella|year=2002|title=Birds of the savannas|url=http://www.epa.qld.gov.au/register/p00820af.pdf|journal=Tropical Topics|volume=73|publisher=Environmental Protection Agency Northern Division|access-date=2008-07-23|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080802095421/http://www.epa.qld.gov.au/register/p00820af.pdf|archive-date=2 August 2008}} the species is considered not threatened, but in Western Australia, the Twenty-eight subspecies (''B. z. semitorquatus'') gets locally displaced by the introduced [[rainbow lorikeet]]s that aggressively compete for nesting places.{{cite web|url=http://www.agric.wa.gov.au/pls/portal30/docs/FOLDER/IKMP/PW/VP/BIRD/LORIKEETMISCPUB.PDF |title=The status and impact of the Rainbow Lorikeet (Trichoglossus haematodus moluccanus) in South-West Western Australia |access-date=2007-11-08 |work=Wildlife Branch, Department of Conservation and Land Management |last=Chapman |first=Tamra |year=2005 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080411043226/http://www.agric.wa.gov.au/pls/portal30/docs/FOLDER/IKMP/PW/VP/BIRD/LORIKEETMISCPUB.PDF |archive-date=11 April 2008}} The rainbow lorikeet is considered a pest species in Western Australia and is subject to eradication in the wild.{{cite web|last=Massam |first=Marion |year=2007 |url=http://www.agric.wa.gov.au/content/pw/vp/bird/pn2006_rainbcontrol_mmassam.pdf |title=Rainbow lorikeet management options |publisher=Department of Agriculture and Food |access-date=2007-11-07 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080411043216/http://www.agric.wa.gov.au/content/pw/vp/bird/pn2006_rainbcontrol_mmassam.pdf |archive-date=11 April 2008 }} In Western Australia, a licence is required to keep or dispose of more than four Port Lincoln ringnecks.{{cite web|url=http://www.rirdc.gov.au/reports/NAP/97-26.pdf|date= February 1997|title=Sustainable Economic Use of Native Australian Birds and Reptiles|access-date=2008-08-02|publisher=[[RIRDC]], Australian government |archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20071026230921/http://www.rirdc.gov.au/reports/NAP/97-26.pdf |archive-date = 26 October 2007}} All four subspecies are sold in the [[Canary Islands]] and in Australia, and they are traded via the [[CITES]] convention.{{cite web|url=http://www.ssn.org/Meetings/cop/cop12/Proposals/SNNDigest_SP12_EN.pdf|title= CITES Digest|date= November 2002|access-date=2008-08-02}} The sale of the Cloncurry parrot is restricted in Queensland.{{cite web|url=http://www.legislation.qld.gov.au/LEGISLTN/SLS/1997/97SL436.pdf|title=NATURE CONSERVATION LEGISLATION AMENDMENT REGULATION (No. 2)|year= 1997|publisher=Queensland, Australia}} The Australian ringneck can suffer from [[psittacine beak and feather disease]], which causes a high nestling mortality rate in captivity.{{cite web|url=http://fedlaw.gov.au/ComLaw/Legislation/LegislativeInstrument1.nsf/framelodgmentattachments/81FCCCA0AB589760CA25718E00044623|year=1999|title=Environment Protection and Biodiversity Conservation Act, Section 270B, Making of Threat Abatement Plans|publisher=Commonwealth of Australia}} ==References== {{reflist}} ===Cited text=== *{{cite book |title=Australian Parrots in Field and Aviary |edition=2nd |last=Lendon |first=Alan H.|year=1973 |publisher=Angus and Robertson |location=Sydney |isbn=0-207-12424-8}} ==Further reading== {{Spoken an electronic format at [http://nla.gov.au/nla.aus-f4773 http://nla.gov.au/nla.aus-f4773] ** B. z. semitorquatus [http://nla.gov.au/nla.aus-f4773-5-s43-e plate] {{Commons category|Barnardius zh.extract:
黑头环颈鹦鹉（學名：Barnardius zonarius，英語：Australian Ringneck、Port Lincoln Parrot，台湾作林肯港環頸鸚鵡），又稱林肯港鸚鵡、澳洲環頸鸚鵡、伯納德氏鸚鵡，是鸚形目舊世界鸚鵡科環頸鸚鵡屬下的唯一一種鳥類。該物種分布於除澳洲除東部、北部及塔斯馬尼亞以外的絕大多數地區，是一種常見的鸚鵡。
該物種由英國動物學家乔治·肖於1805年描述，歸於非洲灰鸚鵡屬下。1854年，法國鳥類學家夏爾·呂西安·波拿巴建立了環頸鸚鵡屬，並將Barnardius typicus指定為模式種，該物種即為現今的B. z. barnardi。其屬名Barnardius來自於尼古拉斯·艾爾沃德·維戈斯與托馬斯·霍斯菲爾德於1827年命名的Platycercus barnardi，其種小名用以紀念英國動物學家兼當時的直轄殖民地管理代理人愛德華·巴納德。種小名zonarius則來自拉丁語「zonarius」，意為「有帶狀物的」。

目前林肯港環頸鸚鵡下有四個亞種。但這個物種曾經被歸入玫瑰鸚鵡屬，也曾被視作兩個物種，分別是林肯港環頸鸚鵡及「馬利鸚鵡」（學名：Barnardius barnardi），但現在已被合併。

林肯港環頸鸚鵡是一種中等體型的綠色鸚鵡，不論亞種皆在後頸處有一道狹窄的黃色領圈，其特徵是展開時長而寬的扇形尾巴。其中東部的亞種頭部顏色為綠色；而中、西部的亞種頭部則以黑色為主。雌雄鳥之間幾乎沒有差別，而幼鳥的顏色較黯淡。其體長33—38公分，體重平均約105—200公克之間。而鳥喙寬平均約14.3公釐、深平均約26.3公釐、嘴峰長平均約22.8公釐；翼長平均約169.2公釐；跗蹠約21.7公釐；尾長約204.4公釐。
牠們主要棲息在開闊的森林、林地、灌木叢和乾旱的荒地上，喜歡成對或小群體活動。林肯港環頸鸚鵡為植食性，多於地面上撿食種子或以挖掘的方式尋找食物。其繁殖季約在8月至隔年1月之間，每次產4—7枚卵，19—20天後孵化，約5周後幼鳥可離巢。
雖然這個物種的族群尚未估計，但被稱作是在澳洲小麥田中最常見的鳥類。而其族群趨勢正可能因其適宜的棲息地正在增加而上升中。《國際自然保護聯盟瀕危物種紅色名錄》中因其相當大的棲地範圍及預期正在上升的族群趨勢，將林肯港環頸鸚鵡列為無危物種。
